Implementing the ⊂PH0⊃ trait allows you to customize the behavior of the
_dereference operator_ ⊂PH1⊃ (not to be confused with the multiplication or glob
operator). By implementing ⊂PH2⊃ in such a way that a smart pointer can be
treated like a regular reference, you can write code that operates on
references and use that code with smart pointers too.

Let’s first look at how the dereference operator works with regular references.
Then, we’ll try to define a custom type that behaves like ⊂PH0⊃ and see why
the dereference operator doesn’t work like a reference on our newly defined
type. We’ll explore how implementing the ⊂PH1⊃ trait makes it possible for
smart pointers to work in ways similar to references. Then, we’ll look at
Rust’s deref coercion feature and how it lets us work with either references or
smart pointers.

⊂PH4⊃. We can assert that ⊂PH5⊃ is equal to ⊂PH6⊃. However, if we want to make an
assertion about the value in ⊂PH7⊃, we have to use ⊂PH8⊃ to follow the reference
to the value it’s pointing to (hence, _dereference_) so that the compiler can
compare the actual value. Once we dereference ⊂PH9⊃, we have access to the
integer value ⊂PH10⊃ is pointing to that we can compare with ⊂PH11⊃.

The third case is trickier: Rust will also coerce a mutable reference to an
immutable one. But the reverse is _not_ possible: Immutable references will
never coerce to mutable references. Because of the borrowing rules, if you have
a mutable reference, that mutable reference must be the only reference to that
data (otherwise, the program wouldn’t compile). Converting one mutable
reference to one immutable reference will never break the borrowing rules.
Converting an immutable reference to a mutable reference would require that the
initial immutable reference is the only immutable reference to that data, but
the borrowing rules don’t guarantee that. Therefore, Rust can’t make the
assumption that converting an immutable reference to a mutable reference is
possible.
